understanding so I hope this meeting is worthwhile for one week waiting so let us dive into it as said the principle of authority and for me this principle is the most fascinating principle and perhaps also the principle that a lot of people don't really get many people get yes I need to be an expert but the main thing is how people perceive you as an expert and that's the fascinating part for me because that's far from rational so for example and some of you might know this study so I will briefly mention it is in a hospital somebody calls to the hospital telling nurses I'm a doctor they never spoke to the man they never hear this name he said I'm doctor was and for this patient you have to give him this drug that patient give him this drug the nurses knew this drug is not good for the patient it will harm the patient still 95 percent of the nurses was going to give the drug to the patient why somebody told it was a doctor and I think this this is the most important to realize on the principle of authority it's not being an expert is being perceived as an expert of course you want to be an expert if you present yourself as an expert that's the ethical component but the mechanisms in our decision making don't check this expertise I always make the joke whoever asked the diplomas of their general doctor nobody does it but maybe you have complaints and that doctor was on the university and he really was well really was bad in this specific topic for you it could be a matter of life or death we never check his credentials and again it's a matter of could be a matter of life or death but that's the fascinating part for me how this principle operates in our brain and therefore also how can you use this for your clients so the first big takeaway I think when you think about using the principle of authority don't think about becoming an expert that's what many people do it's about how can you be perceived as the expert and how that works in our system one decision making and that's what we will dive into the definition like I said we rely on those with superior knowledge or wisdom and an interesting question is why do we do that and the answer is pretty basic because we don't want or we don't have the time to learn it we need that shortcuts we cannot learn everything so we decide we need an expert as you all know in the nigh of school motors model authority reduces uncertainty just that social proof one other takeaway for today authority is often more powerful than social proof before they amplify together social proof works but authority has a big impact on our brain and I like to introduce this study you might hear me telling about this before but I want to re-raise it to your awareness what happened people participated in a research and they were asked to think about their financial situation and make very complicated decisions about what would happen if one of one of you would pass away if you cannot work anymore how do you fund your studies of your children big financial questions and imagine brain activity and they saw a lot of activity people were really putting their mind to work now something happened the researcher came to those people with another person and he told those people I have here person XYZ this person helped thousands of people to make the best financial decisions built a sustainable financial model for those people he won awards of best financial planner of the year and then he started helping those people and they looked at brain activity it was almost reduced to zero so what the principle of authority does to us when we decide to see somebody as the expert we stop thinking for ourselves so if you connect this to being persuasive that's powerful at the most as if we can have a mechanism that people stop thinking for themselves and we lie on us perceiving us as an expert then you can move forward in basically anything financial decisions are having a huge impact but what happened here and that's why this fascinates me there was a researcher that people didn't know that person pretty well so a person you don't know introduce another person you never met he said a few lines about that person and we stopped thinking for ourselves we call this introductions we also say this is the most easily correctable mistake in persuasion and many people are often not introduced and they don't introduce others why introduce others we know another principle reciprocity if you start doing that other people will start introducing you but there's a way of how should what there are three and we only talk basically about a very simple concept but sometimes simple concept can be very hard to execute at the best level so that was also my idea to talk about this for a pretty lengthy period because I promise you this helps you so stop thinking for yourself that we want people stop thinking for themselves perceiving us as the expert or our clients as an expert and now the second thing what truly fascinates me is how powerful that is and how that happens so I think we all know that so I don't go in a very lengthy story but we I shared it in the lunch and learns it's Milgram's experiment why I re-raise why I still want to talk about it because it was so controversial back then the Nazi hunters were very active it was in the early 60s and Eichmann Adolf Eichmann was just captured by the Mossad and brought to trial in Israel and then this researcher comes out and he said well maybe Adolf Eichmann wasn't that bad of a person maybe he wasn't that evil maybe he was just following orders so a strong flood of critics came to Milgram but of course he was prepared for that critic he wanted that critic to open people's eyes because he already did his research and what he did he put an ad this is actually the original ad he said I do a memory study and to participate like I just said I do this a little bit quicker than I can talk about to study also for the next 30 minutes I think you want other information so what he said it up we have three roles we have the doctor the researcher basically we have the student and we have the teacher and we pick a role so whether you are a student or a teacher in reality everybody became the teacher and what's said if people make a mistake they get an electric shock 15 volt every mistake they make 15 volt is added to it so 30 15 30 45 and that went up to 450 volt 200 volt it really hurts 350 volts is a line you can pass out 450 volts is often lethal so you can you die that was being told to those people to the teachers then before they entered the room they got an electric shock from 15 volts so they that hurts if you get that on your finger ah it's not a nice feeling so they knew 15 volt is already not nice so they were really I would I would say this correctly they understood that if they pressed a button they will hurt somebody well a quick video I don't think I'm sharing the sound what I wanted to show is a person oh really oh thanks Patrick 295 volts the correct one let me out of here slow dance let me out of here you want more I can see me here let me out let me out of here let me out continue please let me out of here let me out of here go on let me out go on let me out go on before you red that is incorrect this will be at 330 correct phrase is rich boy let me out of here my heart's bothering me let me out of here let me out of here let me out of here you're right to hold the next phrase is fast let me out let me out let me bird car train plane eventually the learner pretended to be unconscious don't play the whole video based on time but you see the of course the student was an actor there were no real electric shots you see the message there was very strong emotions remember World War II just ended Nazi hunters were fully active trying to bring the evilest people ever alive to justice and this was in America who were perceived just to save the world he made that claim Milgram his findings only one percent of all people stopped giving a shock after the student asked for it you saw the way it was asked there was not just please can you stop was shouting to stop 66 percent continued to the lethal shock and then they did what you just saw in the video my heart is bothering me you cannot give me shocks I have heart problems still 65 percent continued to the lethal shock so when he got this flood of criticism Milgram shows his study yes even American people that you perceive now as the world saviors are able to do this they can follow orders why I tell this because the question here is like I started this mastermind this is why there was a person in the room with a white coat a person that was perceived as a medical expert probably he knows what to do and you saw in that video you're responsible that's what he said I don't know if everybody hear that line but the researcher said he was not in the video but you hear him talking you're responsible person that's what happened they were not forced of pressing a button they were just instructed by a person in a white coat mark I think you want to mention something yes I would like to if you don't mind quantify that study a little bit because the way Milgram originally put it out there was that people blindly follow orders and subsequently people like Hazel by studied this stuff at uni at some point went through the archive and saw that actually at that final point when you say you must continue people actually stopped that's not to say that the authority figure didn't push people to quite an extent but in his original study he said yeah some people went as bad as that lethal 540 540 volt shock it's not quite that far a lot of people most people in fact just said no no no that's going too far so you could take them to that extent but they weren't going to the point where they were actually physically harming or killing people thank you for that Adam and of course this was super controversial research and it got a lot of criticism because he people also want to prove them wrong we cannot be this kind of species so that's one area and I know this this this spotlight and you're right so I'm happy you say that only how I look at it from my own reasoning seeing how powerful this principle is it still works for me and it's still it actually proves it more versus an authority you see here security team or police people versus Martin Luther King in persuasion we want to be an authority somebody we want to follow like people wanted to follow Martin Luther perceive somebody as an expert we went pretty deep into introductions and how powerful it is so what are examples of people we see as an authority you learn that in the practitioner course so I don't mention them all I want to reflect back to my previous comment why we see a plumber as an expert again we don't have the time or we don't want to make the time to learn that and that's why if my pipes are broken I call a plumber and just because I google plumber and I find the company that says I'm a plumber I'm convinced he is I never checked did he actually study for it or whatever I don't check the facts what I might check is his reviews not the facts that's interesting how this principle works if you really think about it two authorities that I want to talk about first are CEOs because they have both roles and you have many roles that have both in and an authority a CEO is in authority employees have to listen to the CEO and you can be a leader that says why do I need to do that dear CEO the answer is because I say so again do you really want to follow such a person no it's same as the police you have to listen but you don't want so that's not what we talk about that persuasion but you have CEOs you want to follow they're charismatic they have the right vision you think those people know where the company is heading and needs to head to I want to work for that person you want to be that kind of CEO because then you are an authority so it's always good to recognize why this principle is working and how I shift from in to an authority and then the other one and this is a from a persuasion point of view the most funny one parents children grow up and how little children perceive their father or mother oh daddy and mommy they know everything if they're little wow they are my heroes you are an authority then they come become teenagers they think daddy and mommy don't know anything and I will show them how the world works now you're in authority so you only have that often as persuasive mechanism and later on that goes back to an authority depending on how well or not well that middle that middle moment when so it's good to be aware of that but now for me and this is the most fascinating point of the principle of authority you all know it's activated by trappings and trappings for non-native English speakers is a word I think you never use because I never used it before I learned the word but it's a cue a clue or something that you can perceive it's tangible so something that you can observe and guide you they think well that's pretty cryptical boss maybe so let me give you examples a uniform a car a gadget a watch a diploma if you think rationally about those things they are pretty basic elements but these are the drivers to perceive somebody as an expert besides authority sorry introductions it's these elements that in the moment drive us to decide is this person an expert that's why this matters so much and again people make the mistake we are too humble we don't show it we think we are bragging but it's that first interaction with us that makes the shortcut of deciding is this person an expert when we now know doing so make that the say if our target of influence makes the decision that person is an expert they stop thinking for themselves it's by these controllable elements this is very controllable only we don't spend often the time advisor back then because that was not a common thing to actually know what you're talking about back then in the Netherlands anyhow i grew up and we never had a nice car at home and it was a thing drove my new Audi a6 back then on the drive as a young kid of 23 i realized that doesn't work out really well so cars is a cultural thing in the Netherlands we often think i pay for that car other cultures let's say a russian culture for example if you don't have that car with you they don't take you serious so it's a cultural element which you have to understand and you have to know what suits your culture best david please just a very very quick anecdote anecdote on that i used to work with with a colleague who used to she was an eight learning and development agency used to sell services technical words versus those who actually used very simple words to explain the same content concepts and those who were being very complicated using jargon so and so forth were actually perceived to be less intelligent than those who used very simple ways of explaining things well thanks for sharing that research i'm aware actually of that reason and that's amazing right so people because you know the reason why what they followed up so it's perceived as you have to make up for something from a persuasion point of view so because of all the jargon people actually distrust you because they think wow you're throwing with these fancy words here you have to make up for something and therefore they were perceived as less intellectual and this is my own findings knowing the fog behavioral model and ability reducing mental effort making simple i compare system one so i don't say this is factual scientific we do a child of seven that's our operating mechanism of making decisions in our
